steps.title: Recognize the core requirement
steps.content: The claim is that sounds are “directed primarily at predators,” meaning they function as anti-predator signals rather than for intraspecies communication (which the passage mentions as an alternative). To support this claim directly, we need evidence of predators responding to the sounds.

steps.title: Match the evidence to the predator-directed function
steps.content:
Choice C shows chickens and yellow warblers (actual predators) stop attacking when they hear caterpillar sounds.

This demonstrates the sounds have a defensive effect on predators, confirming they function as anti-predator signals.

Choices A, B, and D address frequency characteristics, production consistency, and species specificity respectively, but none establish that predators actually respond to the sounds.

This matches choice C — C is correct.
